summary: Graham argues that beyond math and physics, there are general truths that any intelligent beings would share, and we can use “alien truth” as a heuristic target for finding them. He suggests this search effectively defines the core of philosophy: discovering broadly universal ideas without needing perfect certainty about their universality.
key_points: Alien truth serves as a threshold for the most general truths short of math and physics.
Heuristics like controlled experiments, practice improves skill, and Occam’s razor likely qualify as alien truths.
Using alien truth as a generous heuristic is more useful than requiring certainty.
The idea aligns with Erdos’s “God’s book,” extending universality beyond math.
Philosophy’s core task may be the search for alien truths, regardless of what it is called.
money_quotes: If you're trying to find the most general truths short of those of math or physics, then presumably they'll be those we'd share in common with other forms of intelligent life.
Alien truth will work best as a heuristic if we err on the side of generosity.
If there's such a thing as alien truth, then there's more in God's book than math.
One could even make the case that the search for alien truth is, if not an accurate description of philosophy, a good definition for it.
Whatever we call it, the attempt to discover alien truths would be a worthwhile undertaking.
key_insight: Using the idea of truths shared by any intelligence as a target helps identify the most universal human ideas and may capture the true aim of philosophy.
